4 Comparisons
ALL CONTENT © COPYRIGHT 2011 AND BEYOND | JON BENSON 3X
FORMULAS, INC. | ALL RIGHTS RESERVED | NO DUPLICATION ALLOWED
Jon: 	Our next session is dealing with the fourth section of the 1-2-3
Formula, which is 4 comparisons.
Now, what do I mean by comparisons? That’s a good question.
Comparisons are like analogies, and we’re going to be using different
structures of analogies. The reason why we want to use now
analogies or comparisons is you’re going to want to create an anchor
to something that’s already recognized as a product or a service that
exists, that people know.
So, if I say something like, “The every other day diet is a lot like
Allstate,” I’m creating an anchor, assuming that people like Allstate
Insurance Company.
Now, I don’t say that, because it’s nothing like Allstate. But, you can
create anchors in a lot of different ways, and we’re going to cover
some of those ways right now, and we’re going to cover the 4 types of
comparisons you want to use.
There are 2. You’re going to use 2 heavy comparisons, 2 serious
comparisons, and 2 light, humorous, light-hearted comparisons.
One is to really drive home a point (obviously, the heavy comparison)
and one is to break the tension and also to create a slight pattern
interrupt.
The goal behind this is to basically anchor something that already
exists to a point that you’re making. It’s that simple. And they’re going
to be sprinkled throughout your video sales letter.

-- 1 of 5 --

2
Don’t worry about where these go right now. All I want you to worry
about is just creating the 4 comparisons. They’re going to be super-
easy to do, using this comparison formula.
I think where they go, we’re going to cover in the next segment, and I
don’t think anyone is going to have a problem with seeing where they
go once, they see the outline of everything.
Let’s talk about these 4 comparisons.
There’s 2 heavy comparisons. First, of the heavy, is you want to
compare your product, service and/or benefits to your primary
competition. That’s really pretty straightforward.
Example: “Unlike competition’s name, my product or service allows
you to blank.” And whatever that is, it’s different. That’s really all you
have to say. That’s pretty straightforward.
The comparison is really important because, ideally, you’re going to
anchor to something that people already know.
When you create an anchor to anything, people start associating you
with the popularity angle of the anchor. In other words, if I keep
saying, over and over again, that my book is like something positive, I
would think of a positive company that most people like. “My product
is a lot like Apple, or my product is a lot like fill-in-the-blank,” something
that I know that my audience primarily would like.
And I’ll keep comparing it to that until, sooner or later, subconsciously,
they think of my product in the same category as Apple, or the same
category as Allstate, or in the same category as whatever I’m
comparing it to.
We’ll show you some specifics here in a second.
But, that’s the basic thing, and it’s really about price, speed, service,
attributes, benefits. That’s what you want to think about.
So, “Unlike my competition, my product gives you a much better price,”
or “my product is much faster,” or “we offer superior service.”
Whatever the attributes of your company or product is that is different,
that stands out, you want to point out.
And remember, if you will, in the last session video we talked about the
Budweiser analogy, how Budweiser just mentioned the fact that they
were beechwood-aged beers, but primarily all beers are beech-wood
aged.

-- 2 of 5 --

3
Sometimes, it’s just as simple as pointing out something that people
may not know. That’s always an attribute. So, think of things in that
nature to come up with your heavy comparison.
The second heavy comparison is more psychologically powerful for
your client, and that is to compare your customers life with your
product and service and without it. This is like a negative dream story.
You want to show them what their life is going to be like with it, and
what their life is going to be like without.
This is usually used in the end of the video sales letter, by the way,
toward the close, just to let you know.
Here’s an example: “When you pick up blank,” whatever product or
service that you have, “you will finally have a solution to blank that will
last you the rest of your life.” You say something as simple as that.
This is the other side, the and without. “Or, you can keep doing exactly
what you’re doing now,” and describe their outcome for not taking
action. Their outcome might just be horrible, “and you’ll just keep
struggling. You’ll never have the freedom and peace of mind and joy
that you deserve. You’re left without your biggest desire,” whatever
that biggest desire is, “You’re left without knowing exactly what to do
for your next party,” or “left without knowing exactly how to write that
letter to a creditor.” “You’re left with constant tension, worrying about
blank.” “You’re going to keep struggling with your weight for the rest of
your life.”
That is the comparison. It’s very simple. It’s a heavy comparison. It’s
your customers life before and after your product. You definitely want
to have one that just covers the before part of it. You don’t have to go
into a long sequence here. You can just say, “You’ll finally have a
solution to blank. And this solution is solid, proven, it’s going to last the
rest of your life, and you never have to worry about it again. Or, you
can take the other alternative, which is you just keep doing what you’re
doing now.” And then you describe what they’re doing now.
Here’s the conclusion to this. You want to exit this by saying
something to this effect: “I don’t want this for you, and I know that you
don’t want this for yourself, because you deserve better.”
Psychologically, this sets your reader up in a powerful way. When you
say “you deserve better,” and you’re anchoring that “deserving better”
to your product, again, deserving better product, it creates a very
positive anchor to your product or service.
“I don’t want this for you because I know you don’t want it for yourself,
and you deserve better. No one wants to struggle and do without

-- 3 of 5 --

4
freedom and peace of mind,” etc. So, you’re listing things that no one
would want. You don’t want it for them, you deserve better.
That’s as simple as it gets: the 2 heavy comparisons.
Let’s get onto the light comparisons, now.
The first light comparison is one that’s very light-hearted but general,
and general being the operative word here. I want you to think of any
tip that you’ve given, or anything that your product delivers or your
service delivers, and then anchor that to an object or thing that your
prospect already knows.
Remember, this is light-hearted and it’s general.
Here’s what I mean: “Weight loss is a lot like blank.” “Dating probably
reminds you of blank.” The most obvious example is, “Life is like a box
of chocolates.” That is a light-hearted comparison. It’s very general.
That doesn’t say anything specific.
Here’s one that we actually used. “Dating is a lot like going to Vegas.
The house always seems to win. In our case, that house is ran by hot
women.” That’s great for men, in a negative way. That’s a negative,
humorous comparison. It’s negative, but it’s light. It’s not a real big,
heavy thing.
“It’s a lot like going to Vegas: the house always win, and that house is
ran by hot women. They always seem to beat us at this game, but
we’re going to show you how to win.”
That’s kind of the driving force that you want behind the light
comparison. It’s very easy to do. Whatever your service or product is
may remind you of blank, and it can be something as obvious as
Vegas.
I’ve often said that “weight loss is like a yo-yo.” I actually will cover that
analogy here, in just a moment. I’ll think of something that is an object
that I can anchor it to, that makes total sense.
So, think of something that your service or product is like, or the
success thereof is like, when they go through it and they get success,
or the lack of success, which is what we did with the dating product.
Lack of success with dating. It’s like going to Vegas. It’s gambling.
The house always wins. But we made it funny. We made it light-
hearted. That’s the key.

-- 4 of 5 --

5
Your second comparison is one that’s light-hearted, but it’s specific to
a tip. It’s basically the same formula, but it’s specific to one of the tips
that you’ve already given in your VSL.
Now, you’ve already created your tips at this point, remember. You’ve
already created your tips. It’s going to make them laugh or think or get
an “Ah, okay, I get it. That’s kind of cool.” That’s all you really want
out of this.
Here’s an example. I’m going to use my specific tip that we talked
about in the early video. “Think of staggering your calories like a yo-
yo; up, down, up, down. This form of yo-yo dieting is actually a good
thing. Think of it as a yo-yo that does tricks.”
That’s basically as simple as that is. I basically am saying, “Stagger
your calories, it’s up and down, but there’s a negative term in dieting
called yo-yo dieting. Most people know about that term. This kind of
yo-yo dieting is actually good. It’s actually a good thing. So, I flipped it
on it’s head, because this is a yo-yo that does tricks.”
And then, I can keep going. I said, “And the trick behind everything is
to learn about the calorie-staggering principle which is covered in my
book.”
That’s the ideas behind the comparisons. They’re very simple to do,
and they can be a lot of fun.

-- 5 of 5 --
